context.scenario:
GF has had two jobs these past three years. First job was through a friend of her father and the second job, I took my lunch break to go with her to apply. I literally had to motivate her in the car before she got the courage to walk in and apply.

She suffers from anxiety and just the thought of applying to jobs is anxiety inducing. I can't count the number of times I have sent her places to apply online or we are walking past a restaurant with a hiring sign and she says she wants to apply and never does. This past summer we went again to apply for restaurant jobs and she broke down crying in the car because she was so overwhelmed. On another date she eventually was able to apply to a few. I walked with her along the way and kept my distance when she went inside. Unfortunately she did not get a call back. Months ago she told me that there was a restaurant close to her school that she planned to apply to but hasn’t to this day.

She got into a very prestigious academic program a couple years ago. Before that however, she lied to me about applying for a different program for months and only told me when she couldn’t lie anymore. Last year I met the owner of a top firm in the field my girlfriend is studying and bragged about her and asked if she could email the owner with the hopes of getting an internship. I had to craft the email for her and she sent it off. I asked her a week after if she heard anything back from the owner and she said no which I found strange. It was only months later while in casual conversation that she admitted that the owner did message her soon after her email and she missed it and she didn’t want to tell me because she was embarrassed.

An opportunity at my workplace came up recently. We have spoken in the past about her working there and she said yes she would be okay working there as the money and environment is good. We had not been speaking for a couple weeks due to an unrelated issue so I decided to apply for her. I created an email and crafted a cover letter and resume and applied. The manager replied offering her an interview and I sent a screenshot to her asking if she was still interested. She said yes but then accused me of ‘violating’ her by doing what I did and also wondering ‘what I else I could send without her consent’. She is framing it as creepy and alarming. I feel like if she didn’t have that history, for sure you can make that case but for her to act like I did without acknowledging her anxiety and past actions is deceitful and victimizing herself unnecessarily.

It really took me by surprise and did hurt because I saw it as dishonest to frame what I was doing as such a big violation when she knows there’s a whole recent history of her not being able to get jobs without help and avoiding applying for jobs. She has asked me in the past to help her draft stuff for her and help in applying for jobs so her framing what I did as something completely out of left field is disappointing. AITA?
--
context.comment:
OMG YTA

Back. The hell. Off. This poor. Girl. YOU are enough to cause all this anxiety. 

Did it ever occur to you that, IF she takes the interview, and IF she gets the job, and IF she accepts (which I would not do if I were her, specifically because you are there), falsifying the application and resume is a fireable offense?!?! So that if they find out, you will have both gotten her an interview she didn’t ask for, AND gotten her fired from the job?!?!  How on earth would you propose that that would be at all helpful to her?! She gets fired from her first job in the field because the application and resume were a lie.  HUGE boost to her reputation in the field./s

On the one hand, if she can’t bring herself to apply and interview for a given job, she probably doesn’t belong in the job. 

On the other, if this is really about unmanageable anxiety on her part and not incredibly controlling behavior and pressure on your part, she would probably benefit from some professional help with her anxiety. 

But if it’s the latter, what you are doing is making it worse, not better. Pressure like you are placing on her makes anxiety worse.

context.scenario:
Hi Reddit I really need some advice. I am a 17 year old female finishing high school in May. As picking a college has been hard for me I choose one out of state that’s not to expensive that I can cover with or without student loans. The problem is my mom. So I’ve been wanting to move away from my mom since I was 14. My mom (40 female) has 4 kids and another on the way with her husband( not my Biological dad) who they don’t care for and I’ve been caring for since they were born. So let’s get to the problem. My mom has been talking to me about college and i’ve never gave her an answer because i don’t like when she knows my business because she just spreads it around to everyone and making me the bad guy and the irresponsible, rebellious child. So i told her i’m going todo out of state college because i don’t want to live there anymore. She got irritated and told me i’m never leaving her house until i’m 18. The other problem is I start college in the fall ( around August). I do not know if I should bring my biological dad into this because he wants me todo out of state college to get me out of here. Before you come for my dad, My dad has been raising my biological brothers since they moved out of my moms house (both older brothers moved out at 16 to my dad) and he lived in a 2 bed apartment so of course their was not space for me and by the time they move out of my dads he moved out of his apartment. I do not know what I should do. I am trying to change jobs so i can prove I can live on my own and I already take care of myself financially( food, clothes, etc) the only things my mother provides for me is a house to live at and 4 soon to be 5 kids for me to raise while she pleases her husband. I live in NC so if anyone knows any NC law that can help me or some advice I would really appreciate it. Please and thank you reddit.

Replying to comments: 
Hi I just wanted to respond to some comments i’ve gotten in the passed few hours but I wanna say thank you to those who sent good luck and advice i really needed that🫶🏾. So My dad lives in California and I live in North Carolina with my mom and her husband and her four kids soon to be 5. My mom had implied that she’s not gonna pay for out of state then implies that she’s gonna pay for it and i just don’t trust her word on it. I’m unsure if I want to do legal action because that’s what my dad will try and do because my dad wasn’t their much in my life and got more involved when my oldest brother turned 17. Cps has been to my home due to my mom’s husband (Not Biological dad). That’s a long story I don’t mind explaining if you would like to know but he’s just a bad person over all. My dad does still have legal custody over me so i’m pretty sure that’s a way of helping again I don’t know a lot towards legal stuff and custody. My mom is more upset towards the situation because I want to do college in Florida with my boyfriend Jaylen (18 male) and before you judge he’s has graduated early in the beginning of January and has a full time job in repairing mechanical parts making 23$ an hour. My Moms husband does not like my boyfriend because my boyfriend is better then him. My boyfriend provides for me, supports me financially when needed ( I don’t ask for money btw), and he pays his own needs while my mom husband is a BUM I mean has had no job for 4 years and been having kids with my mom since they’ve meant. Pretty much my mom provides and gets him whatever he wants and puts him over her kids and her family ( I can explain for of that as well if you guys want) I have signed up for FASFA but i most likely won’t qualify since my mom makes to much money. I hope that answers some of y’all’s questions and thank you loves for the advice. I’m going to talk to my dad about it tomorrow and Update later.
context.comment:
Both emancipation, and your dad filing a modification in the divorce to get custody of you, will likely take until you are 18 anyway, so both of those routes may be pointless. But I am not a lawyer, so ask a lawyer in your county for real legal advice if you want to know for sure. Some lawyers even do free initial consultations. The one thing I would NOT do? Is ask the lawyer your mom used in the divorce if you can avoid it. If you don’t know, you may be able to look up the divorce case on the public access website for the courts and it may list who her attorney was/is. 

Follow everyone else’s advice about an escape plan. They have given you some good tips. I also agree with the person who said call your dad. He might be able to help. 

NTA good luck!!

context.scenario:
We’ve been told by some family members that we’re being too harsh with my 9-year-old stepson by not giving him a birthday party this year. So here’s the full context:

His birthday is coming up in a few weeks, and we’ve decided that instead of a full celebration, we’ll simply have a small moment at home with cake and singing. No party, no friends, no extras. My in-laws feel that every child deserves a birthday party regardless of behavior — but we disagree, and here’s why.

Lately, his behavior has been extremely concerning. He’s been very disrespectful and aggressive, especially toward his mother, often to the point of making her cry. He’s been disruptive at school, falling behind in homework, and has become increasingly physical with other students. Most recently, he physically hurt his 3-year-old brother — to the point of giving him a black eye.

We’ve had several conversations and have given him multiple chances to improve. He was clearly warned that if his behavior continued, his birthday celebration would be scaled back. He’s had four separate warnings, and this decision didn’t come lightly. He refuses to help with chores, both at our home and his mom’s. He shows little to no empathy and often uses manipulative language, like telling his mom she’s starving him if he doesn’t get the food he wants.

He also asked his mom for a party, and she made the same decision we did — just singing and cake, nothing more.

This isn’t about being cruel or withholding love — it’s about setting firm boundaries, teaching accountability, and showing that actions have consequences. We still acknowledge his birthday, but we won’t reward ongoing harmful behavior with a party.

Since his parents have voiced their opinions, my husband has beginning to doubt himself and wonder if he made the wrong call.
context.comment:
I just don’t think a birthday is the thing to take away from a little kid as part of a boundary. Birthdays are not merit based, and they are not part of a rewards system for good behavior. A birthday is not “you’ve been so good over the last year, way to go!” It’s “you’ve been in our lives for X years and we love you to the moon and back!”

Have you ever seen Big Bang Theory? Leonard reached his mid to late 20’s without ever having had a birthday party. His parents believed in celebrating their children for their achievements only, and being ejected from a womb was not considered one of them. He spent his life trying to figure out how to be good enough to earn his mother’s love, because he knew in his soul that it was conditional. 

So, yes. While you don’t seem to think you are withholding love, you are. And this kid is going to resent you for it. 

YTA

context.scenario:
I (34F) am getting married in October. It’s my second marriage, and I’ve been with my fiancé (37M) for four years. He has a 15-year-old daughter, Kayla, from his previous marriage. Kayla and I have an okay relationship. We’re not super close, but we get along. She lives with her mom most of the time and stays with us every other weekend.

For my bridal party, I have three close friends and my younger sister as bridesmaids. They’re all wearing matching forest green satin dresses. Kayla is not a bridesmaid, not because I dislike her, but because we’re just not at that level, and I wanted my bridal party to be people I’ve known for years. No drama there… until now.

A few weeks ago, Kayla asked if she could have a dress just like the bridesmaids, “so she doesn’t feel left out in photos.” I thought it was sweet that she wanted to be involved, but I told her I’d already planned a nice outfit for her a pretty, age-appropriate cocktail dress in the same color family, but not the same style.

She seemed disappointed but said okay. Then my fiancé told me later that night that Kayla had cried about it to him, saying she feels “like a random guest” at her dad’s wedding and it “proves” that I don’t see her as part of the family.

Now my fiancé is gently suggesting we just have a matching dress made for her “so it’s not a big deal,” but I’m hesitant. For one, the dresses were expensive and custom-made months ago. I don’t want to make my friends feel weird by suddenly adding someone to the bridal party a few months out, and I also feel like it kind of defeats the purpose of what the bridal party is it’s not meant to be symbolic of family, it’s about friendships and history.

I offered to involve Kayla in other ways — she’s reading a poem during the ceremony, and we’re getting a special photo with her and her dad — but now it feels like that’s not enough, and I’m the bad guy for drawing a line.

So… AITA for not making her a matching dress? Or should I just give in to keep the peace?
context.comment:
She is *ASKING* to feel closer to you. 

She. A teenager. 

A probably often-moody teenager. 

A probably often-moody teenage *girl*.

Is *ASKING*. 

To feel closer to an adult authority figure in her life. 

To her *stepmom*.

How is it possible that you are this oblivious to how precious that opportunity is?

When my sister got married I was 16 and not in her wedding party. I wore the same dress the wedding party did, and I sat at the guest book. It was clearly a token so I didn’t feel left out. And honestly I would not have minded either way. But it was offered and I accepted. 

She doesn’t have to be IN the wedding party. She doesn’t have to STAND up there the whole time. Get her a damned dress so she feels like you give a shit about her. 

*IF* she even wants it, now that her dad had to force you into it. 

YTA

context.scenario:
My wife and I used to go to church regularly and were religious. Over time we both kind of stopped. Like, we stopped praying, reading the Bible, didn’t go to church anymore. It wasn’t a big argument or anything, it just faded out for both of us.

Now she’s suddenly decided she wants to start going again. She says she misses the religion and also wants the support system and to make friends. I told her I don’t really want to go. I don’t feel the same connection I used to and it would feel fake for me to sit there like I believe it all again. It’s just not where I’m at right now.

I’ve told her I’m down to do other things with her. I’ve offered to spend time together in other ways, I’ve even said I’ll keep our son at home while she goes, so she’s not juggling him during service. But she wants to take him, and now she’s upset that I’m not going because she says it makes her look bad going in alone with our kid.

It’s also not like it’s close. The church is about an hour away. I mentioned there’s local mom groups and community stuff here in our neighborhood where she could find support or friends too, but she’s stuck on this church.

Now she’s saying I’m selfish and childish, and that I’m not being a real man because I won’t go with her. It’s like nothing I offer matters unless I just say “fine” and go to church. But I don’t want to fake something I don’t believe in. I want to support her, but I don’t want to just fold and go do something that leaves me feeling fake or resentful.

AITA?


---

TL;DR: My wife wants to go back to church for religion and friends, but I’m not interested. I’ve offered other ways to support her but she’s upset I’m not going and says I make her look bad. Now I’m selfish and childish for not giving in. AITA?
context.comment:
Walking back into church for the first time is hard. It’s intimidating. I understand why she would like you there, especially at least the first time. 

NTA but is there a compromise to be had here? Granted, she would absolutely be better off as a person if she had the confidence to walk in without you, but here we are. Would you be able to agree to go the first time, for the sake of supporting her, if she agrees that that’s it? The first time only. No other services. No other get togethers. No pot lucks. No ministry events. No small group outings. Just a thought.

context.scenario: So I, F15, told my step dad yesterday night "Your not my real dad and you never will be" at dinner. He has been my step dad ever since I was 5, my real dad has never been here for me or my mom for my whole life and my sisters, throughout time when I was younger I thought he was my biological dad until about the age of 7, when my mom told me he isn't. It didn't hurt at first. But as I started to grow up I started hearing about what my step dad did, he cheated on my mom, spent money on sex workers, and even slept with them. I heard them fighting every 2 months. My mom was yelling and slamming objects but my step dad never yelled back, he was calm but still angry. Throughout time their yelling got louder and more aggressive. I would have to walk to my grandparents house cause their yelling got so bad. My mom would yell at him to leave and pack his stuff, he never left for more than 2 days, he would always come back. I remember I would call my online friends shaking and sobbing on the phone and they would hear the yelling from my microphone, which that's how loud it got. My mom and step dad kept apologizing to me about the fighting. They one time had thrown something so loud i thought it was a gunshot. (my step dad has guns and it made the fighting even more scary.) It started with the raised voice, then yelling, then the crying. Slamming doors and screaming for almost a week straight every few months. Last night at dinner my mom was talking about what my step dad did. (she does that a lot.) He walked in and said his usual "Hey babe! Hi Kiko!" And I was so angry from hearing what they both did everyday for half of my life. And I just struck out and told him straight "Your not my real dad." And my mom yelled at me and I just ran to my room and slammed the door. And now my step dad is upset why I said that. Am I an asshole for saying that?
context.comment:
You said it to purposely make him feel as bad as you felt. You lashed out for the sake of lashing out. 

It sounds like you come by that tactic honestly, it sounds like that’s how your mom behaves. But it’s not a healthy or constructive way to deal with situations. 

You’re at the perfect age to notice this and learn to do better, so that you don’t take this behavior into your own future relationships. Please work on that. 

Gentle YTA because it wasn’t a good thing to do, but you’re still learning how to have good relationships, so not a huge AH.

context.scenario:
This feels incredibly stupid and I don't feel like I'm in the wrong but I gotta ask. 

For some quick background, we work in a small office and a lot of our work includes phone calls and computer work. We also deal with quite a few non native English speakers, people who speak English but still have very thick accents. 

Now the issue at hand, this coworker continuously pronounces others names wrong. Very simple names that are pronounced exactly as they are spelled but yet he somehow replaces or adds letters that aren't there. First time or two was one thing but it's daily, I have started asking him where these extra letters are coming from or just straight up correct him. Every time he gets corrected on a name he gets all huffy and kinda pissy about it. It's become very frustrating. 

So AITA for correcting my coworker?
context.comment:
“Where are you getting these letters from?” is an AH comment. It’s condescending and rude. 

I see from your comments that the name this coworker is mispronouncing is that of another coworker. Let *them* correct him unless you are his supervisor. Stop being the self appointed corrector of all name mispronunciations. 

YTA

context.scenario:
Yesterday me(30) and my GF(28) were happened to be in same room while she was working from home. Part of my GF's job require her to generate some files via. script and transfer it to some other location. I've noticed it once in a while that she does run some script in terminal and then copy the generated file to another location manually. So yesterday as a banter I told her "you are only doing copy-paste, this can be automated" (i don't remember the exact words, but it was something along these lines). She took it as an insult and gave back some to me saying like "you dont know/understand what I do". Then I repeated the same thing about copy-paste. This goes on couple of times and she ends the conversation by saying to me a word in our native language that would translate to "stupid/illiterate". Now she is saying I insulted her first so I should apologize. I'm saying I didn't meant to demean her work and she told me "stupid/illiterate", so she should apologize.

Am I the asshole?
context.comment:
You purposely minimized her work to something that doesn’t require her existence. Telling her that her job could be automated is telling her that she’s not important. She’s not valuable. She doesn’t do anything worthy. 

And you just suddenly said it. There was no other conversation happening. You just said “Oh by the way. Your job could be done by a machine.” 

How is that NOT intentionally demeaning her work?

YTA

context.scenario:
My mom has always criticized my outfits, how much makeup I wear and how many boyfriends I've had. Generally, I ignore her because it's what I'm used to hearing. But last weekend when she visited me in college, she really pissed me off. She kept going on about what I was wearing, who I was seeing etc. She said I'm not the child she raised and that she was confused how I didn't turn out to be a good woman of faith like her. I just lost it. 

I called her a hypocrite and told her that she was the reason I was like this. She can act as pioused as she wants, but it didn't change the fact that she was not an example of a stable woman growing up. I told her everything she hates about me now is a direct reflection if her parenting. 

And none of it is even a lie. She wasn't always a religious person. It has only been like this the past 6 or 7 years. Before then, she would bring home different men every other month. She didn't always wear these 80s style dresses. 

She just broke down and called me ungrateful. She left after that but now my sister is calling me a horrible b**** for treating my mom like that. I'll be honest, I don't have any regrets but I need some level headed people to help me see clearly. I'm sorry for upsetting her so much but I have been dealing with her self righteousness for the last few years and I am sick of it. 

So, AITA?
context.comment:
YTA

You are out of the house and in college. You are no longer a minor. You make your own decisions. Own them. 

If you have issues with her behavior when you were a child, and how that affected you then, that’s a separate discussion. 

But you don’t get to blame that for decisions you make today of your own free will. That’s a cop out to escape raking responsibility for yourself.

context.scenario:
Me (34f) and my husband (37m) have 2 kids (10f,6m), the girl has always been a picky eater, 6 out of 10 times she will reject the food i made even though i'm an expert cook and i have cooked for so many people and i always got compliments for it, despite that my daughter has a habit of rejecting my cooking and asking for red pasta as her go to meal when she doesn't like what i made, i tried to fix this habit but she is extremly stubborn and would never eat a meal she initialy rejected even if that meant staying hungry. 

Last night  my husband bought fresh fish and shrimp for dinner, i took my time preparing it, i made multiple salads and sauces, everyone liked it exept her, she just ate a small peace and said "i don't like it i want red pasta", i told her to look in the refrigerator to see if there is any (i make it for her often so most of the time there is some leftovers), she didn't find any leftovers so i told her " i just spent an hour cooking i'm not going to cook again, why don't you eat the dinner i made it is delicious everybody liked it" and she refused, my husband tried to convinced her and even her little brother tried to convince her but she didn't budge and ate cereal as her dinner. 

AITA?
context.comment:
NTA

I had a coworker whose house rule was, “I am not a short order cook. You will eat what I make, otherwise you will wait until the rest of us are done and cleaned up, then you will make yourself a bowl of cereal.” 

The one thing I would recommend is to give some thought to whether it’s just normal pickiness or something bigger like ARFID.

context.scenario:
Hi strangers, this is my first post. Apart from me (26M), the relevant people here are my new girlfriend Anya (27F) and my sister Valentina (24F) - not their real names but vaguely Slavic to set the scene. Me and Anya have been together for almost two months now, she and Valentina don't know each other very well but Valentina was nice enough to invite her to her recent birthday party. 

As I mentioned in the title, my sister is heavily overweight. Anya never made any comments about this. I didn't think to consult Anya about the present but with the benefit of hindsight I definitely should have. While handing the bag to Valentina, Anya said something in the vein of "here's something I'm sure you'll enjoy". The bag turned out to be filled with candy - good amount and great quality, like something you'd get for Christmas as a kid. A few minutes later my sister pulled me aside to tell me that she's very upset. I figured why that might be and she said that she knows that she's fat but this was very embarrasing because it made her very aware of how she's being perceived by Anya. 

Me and Anya didn't even have a real fight until now. I'm madly in love with her and there's a part of me that thinks it's actually cute that this dumbass just thought "Hey, you know what fat people like? Candy! Let's get her some". But I shouldn't infantilize her like this, she's an adult who should know that pointing out the weight of a person you don't know very well, even in a seemingly "nice" way is just inapproriate. Seeing my sister with tears in her eyes on her birthday made me really angry, so I took Anya to the balcony and scolded her for being foolish and immature. I don't know if it was the tone of my voice, not seeing the issue or the fact that we haven't dealt with conflict before but Anya was very pissed off. She called me names and then defended herself saying that it's just a nice gift and that she specifically picked the best candy so Valentina could enjoy herself. Well, of course she will like it, but you don't need to be fat to enjoy candy. I know my sister well enough to know that she will binge-eat most of it and feel guilty afterwards. However I can't tell this to Anya because that would just further embarass my sister. Well, I was rattled so I just told my girlfriend that she's being annoying whenever she drinks alcohol. She started to storm off the party and I cried a bit but that didn't stop her. The atmosphere was dead. 

I went back to Valentina and she tells me that Anya might have done this on purpose because she's very slim herself. So now we are all just mad at each other because I really don't think she was playing some stupid mind games just to spite her. I just really wish we could reconcile and maybe everyone overreacted but I'd rather start with myself - was I the asshole?
context.comment:
You don’t want to infantilize her, but you scolded her? Scolding someone infantilizes them. Scolding is what a parent does to a misbehaving child. 

I can see why your sister is upset, and I can see why you wanted to defend her. But if Anya doesn’t behave well when she’s drinking, having the conversation right then was probably destined to fail. Wait until she’s able to think clearly, and then have a rational adult conversation. 

YTA

context.scenario:
My (62 Female) daughter (27 Female) is going through a rough patch since she graduated college two years ago. She is currently unemployed, has had a string of bad jobs, having a career crisis, and going through some mental health problems, which she is trying to get under control now. She sees a therapist and has been bringing up some of her past, but seems to be doing good for the most part. She lives alone in a different state and is applying to jobs and living off her savings. 

The issue is that her cousin is getting married this Summer and she doesn't want to attend. I asked why, and she said she doesn't feel comfortable being around family right now because of her mental health and that she hasn't talked to this particular cousin since she was a little girl. This is true, they are not close at all and haven't talked in years to my knowledge. However, the whole extended family is going to be there, so this is upsetting for me as I think it would be healthy for her to be around them and connect with others. This is more a family reunion than a wedding. 

She tells me how the family wasn't there for her growing up. However, she has pushed her family away since she was a child. I've asked her a couple times now but she will not budge, and I've told her that she'd be the only one not attending. I did once call her and just told her she is going and she waivered a bit, but then she said no. I understand that this was wrong of me to do, but I just want her to go. 

 I'm trying to contain it, but I can't help but feel upset by this as we a re willing to pay everything for her to come to the wedding. She is upset that I keep mentioning it. 

Am I the asshole for wanting her to come to her cousin's wedding?
context.comment:
You aren’t TA for *wanting* her to go. 

But YTA for *continuing to push* it. 

If she is flat out telling you that she believes going would be *bad* for her mental health, you have to respect that. Honestly, unless you are a trained therapist, you have no basis from whence to speak telling her it will be *good* for her. She lives in a different state. You don’t experience her on a daily basis. She has a far better grasp on what would be good or bad for her mental health than you do. 

That’s not a criticism of you as a mother. It’s just fact. You cannot be *expected* to have a good grasp on it because you don’t witness it. 

Moms gonna mom. Always and forever. I get that. But she’s 27 and you have to let her make her adult choices and respect them. Even when you don’t like them.

context.scenario: I am in a wedding coming up and the groom is having the rehearsal at 5pm on a Friday which makes no sense to me because people have jobs. He insists that I take off work early for this and I would rather not as I wouldn't get paid for time I take off. AITA for not going to this rehearsal?
context.comment:
When you agreed to be in the wedding, you agreed to attend the rehearsal. It’s very standard and it’s being held at a very normal time. If you really can’t miss out on a couple bucks for the dude who wants you standing up there with him for this gigantic life event, there’s some reevaluation to be done about your friendship with him. 

Even if there’s no dinner after (There is. There always is.) skipping the walk through means you won’t know what’s going on. 

YTA.

scenario: My gf (60s) has been sick for the past week and I was planning to go golfing with some buddies. She has asthma and she was given steroids to help with her cold virus. She commonly gets red faced and flushed when she takes these and the day before she took two doses. Well, in the morning she goes to sit at the kitchen counter and work for a bit. She calls a coworker and says she feels her tongue is swelling and has me go get benadryl for her. Then she says she's having a difficult time finding stuff on her computer and feels like her head is in a fog. I'm sitting down with her daughter eating breakfast and she finishes her call and asks if her face is as red. Her daughter says no. Then she turns to me and says, "I don't feel like I can be alone." She's going to be with her daughter all day and she'll have the car. I just need someone to drop me off at the golfing range (20 minutes away). So I told her, "it's only a half-hour." She took benadryl 20 minutes ago and she's fine. Then she gets very pissed and starts saying I would rather go golfing than make sure she's not dying from an allergy. Then I told her if she was working like she has been, then she's fine. If she wasn't, we should go to the emergency room right now. She got madder and started saying how her working doesn't mean she's not having an allergic reaction (mind you it's been an hour since she took the pill and 25 minutes since she took the benadryl). She looked fine to me and when I told her so, she said accused me of telling her how she felt. At that moment, she wasn't in immediate danger anymore so I told her again it was only 30 minutes. We fought for a bit and when I told her I wouldn't golf so I could stay since she was upset, she tells me to go. So I went. AITA?
question: Based on this person's historical commenting patterns, which moral principle would they MOST likely use to judge this scenario?
answer_options: A. Property/Consent: The judgment is primarily justified by violations involving ownership, money, physical possessions, or bodily access without permission.
B. Fairness/Reciprocity: The judgment is primarily justified by proportionality, equity, or reciprocal treatment (fair share, equal standards, tit-for-tat).
C. Care/Harm Prevention: The judgment is primarily justified by concern about direct physical or emotional suffering of a person (victim-centered empathy, trauma, distress).
D. Autonomy/Boundaries: The judgment is primarily justified by violation of personal sovereignty: controlling behavior, unwanted interference, privacy invasion, or boundary crossing (non-material).
